principle: Only initiate a long-vol position (buy options/gamma) when a defensible forecast of realized volatility over the option's life exceeds the implied volatility paid, AND you can name the specific catalyst that will lift realized vol. 'IV looks cheap' alone is not a trade.
mechanism: The entire delta-hedged long-option edge reduces to (1/2)S^2*Gamma*(sigma_realized^2 - sigma_implied^2) per unit time, i.e. you win only when realized variance beats the implied variance baked into the price. Cheap vol is usually cheap for a reason (no near-term catalyst), so absent a catalyst the position just bleeds theta against an efficiently-priced quiet window.

principle: Treat being long vol as fighting a structural variance/equity-risk premium: implied is systematically above subsequent realized, so a passively-held long-vol book bleeds on average. Long vol must be selective (events, cheap regimes) not buy-and-hold, and forecasts should be de-biased by subtracting the 'usual' IV-over-RV spread before judging an option cheap.
mechanism: Sellers earn the VRP as compensation for providing insurance, bearing negative-skew/fat-tail returns, and taking losses that cluster with equity drawdowns. A long-vol position is implicitly short equity risk (vol up when equities fall), so it is expected to underperform the risk-free rate by the equity risk premium on top of the overpricing. Long vol is therefore beta (paying a premium), not alpha.

principle: Time long-vol entries by overlaying current IV on a volatility cone (the historical RV distribution per tenor) and a regime-conditioned RV forecast; buy vol only when IV sits near the LOW percentile of the cone for that horizon, not merely below a single point forecast.
mechanism: A point RV forecast hides the dispersion that long vol monetizes; the cone shows where today's IV sits versus the realized range, and the upper tail of the RV distribution is the long-gamma payoff zone. Buying when IV is low-percentile means RV has room to spike up. Use Hodges-Tompkins overlapping-data adjustment to build cones from limited history.

principle: Condition long-vol exposure on regime: bias long-vega entries to elevated/rising-IV regimes (India VIX above its EWMA) and to bear regimes (index below its 200-day MA where median RV roughly doubles), and avoid passive long index vol in calm low-vol regimes where the premium overpayment is proportionally largest.
mechanism: Vol is right-skewed and spends most time low; median 30-day vol nearly doubles below the 200-day MA (21.6% vs 12.1% for S&P). The proportional variance premium is largest when IV is low and narrows/inverts at high VIX, so calm regimes are the most-taxed time to be long vol. Short-vol does best in stable/declining VIX; the mirror is that long vol works in rising VIX.

principle: Before buying vol over an NSE earnings/event, decompose front-vs-second-month ATM IV into diffusive vol plus event/jump vol, convert jump vol to the implied expected move (E|R| ~ sqrt(2/pi)*sigma_E), and only go long if your forecast move comfortably exceeds the implied jump. Adjust single-stock term structure by the index term structure to strip macro slope.
mechanism: Variance is additive, so the front-month IV equals diffusive variance over (T-1) days plus one day of jump variance; the forward vol between expiries isolates diffusion, and the residual is the event's contribution. If front-month IV is not even above the second month, the market prices no event move and a long straddle just bleeds theta. A steep term structure can make the jump look richer than it is, hence the index adjustment.

principle: Screen long earnings-straddle candidates by analyst earnings-estimate dispersion (high-dispersion names are materially more profitable), not by PE/market-cap/dividend yield/coverage; concentrate long-vol earnings bets on high-disagreement NSE single names.

The relationship is nonlinear (quartile P/L: 3/10/14/48), captured by quartile sorts rather than correlation. Fundamental signals are orthogonal to the time-series RV the market watches, so the edge is less crowded.

principle: The delta-hedged long-gamma P&L engine is: profit ~ (1/2)Gamma*S^2*(realized return^2) minus theta, captured by re-hedging to flat (buy dips, sell rallies). P&L scales with the SQUARE of moves (2x move = 4x profit), so it is a bet on large/jumpy moves; on quiet days you pay theta with no offset.
mechanism: Taylor expansion of a delta-hedged option gives daily P&L = (1/2)Gamma*dS^2 + theta + financing. Gamma scalping crystallizes the convexity gain on each re-hedge. Direction is hedged away — magnitude is what matters. Carry (gamma profit minus theta) is linear in (realized - implied) vol points, roughly equal across low- and high-vol names per unit dollar gamma because gamma ~ 1/sigma.

principle: Do not hedge continuously. Use a no-trade delta band (Zakamouline/Whalley-Wilmott approximation) sized from gamma, cost, and risk aversion; rehedge only when delta leaves the band, and only to the band edge. For LONG-gamma positions widen the band (let deltas run) versus short-gamma, because convexity self-insures adverse moves.
mechanism: Continuous BSM hedging incurs infinite transaction cost; each rehedge crosses the spread for diminishing risk reduction. The HN optimal band is asymmetric — wider for long gamma. WW band scales as (cost*S^2*gamma/risk-aversion)^(1/3); Zakamouline adds a floor term H0 so even far-OTM tail wings get a minimum rehedge band. Monte Carlo shows profit erodes from weekly to 5x-daily hedging (~2% to ~20% shortfall).

principle: Delta-hedge a long-vol position using a delta computed from your EXPECTED (forecast) volatility, not the quoted implied vol — if you bought an option 5 vol points cheap, hedge at implied+5. This matters most for long vol in equities because vol rises as spot falls (skew), so implied-vol deltas systematically over-hedge you long into a decline and bleed the edge.
mechanism: Using the proper (higher) vol gives more time value and a more accurate delta, so gamma-hedge profit reconstructs theoretical-value-minus-price. With negative spot-vol correlation, in a falling market the straddle's strike sits above spot; the implied-vol delta understates time value and leaves you secretly long delta — exactly wrong on the way down. Hedging at realized gives a more certain terminal P&L but a noisier path; hedging at implied gives a smooth path robust to a poor forecast.

principle: Accept that a correct realized-vol forecast does NOT guarantee profit on any single long-gamma trade: discrete-hedging path noise scales as ~sigma*vega*sqrt(pi/4N) and is independent of your edge size; outcomes are highly path-dependent because gamma lives near the strike. Treat each trade as one draw from a wide distribution; rely on repetition and sizing, not single outcomes.
mechanism: Total profit accrues through discrete rebalances, and where price spends its time relative to the strike determines how much variance you actually capture. Two paths with identical realized vol give different P&L. A big move late (near expiry, near strike) is most monetizable; an early jump away from the strike kills your gamma. Hedging-error dispersion is ~1/sqrt(N) under diffusion but jumps cannot be hedged-away by frequency.

principle: For a direction-neutral long realized-vol / event bet, the cleanest retail-accessible structures are long ATM straddles or (cheaper, flatter-vega) OTM strangles. Prefer strangles/strips over a single straddle to keep gamma alive across a wider range, and recognize the true delta-neutral straddle strike sits slightly above spot.
mechanism: A straddle/strangle cancels first-order delta, leaving long gamma/vega that profits on large moves either way. A single straddle loses gamma as spot drifts from the strike; a strangle's flatter vega keeps long-vol exposure stable as the underlying moves. Zero-delta straddle strike = S*exp((r+sigma^2/2)T), above spot.

principle: Match strike to the expected move size: for ordinary directional-with-cheap-vol views use near-the-money/ITM strikes (best risk-adjusted return on normal moves); reserve far-OTM 'lottery' strikes for genuine tail/jump bets. Avoid the maximum-elasticity (deep-OTM) strike trap — it requires being almost exactly right and far-OTM long calls have the WORST expected returns.
mechanism: ITM options behave like a forward (high delta, little time value) and capture most of a normal move; OTM options have low delta so a normal move barely moves them, and only an abnormal/jump move makes their cheap convex leverage dominate. Far-OTM call buyers are lottery-seeking and systematically overpay; deeper OTM = worse expected return.

principle: Delta-hedge index long-vol positions with index futures (NIFTY/BANKNIFTY), which are financing-costless and avoid dividend exposure; hedge single-stock options with the matched-maturity stock future, not stock, to strip out dividend/borrow risk. Hedging with a mismatched-maturity instrument or with stock introduces dividend risk proportional to delta.
mechanism: A forward/future excludes dividends that go ex before expiry, matching the option (which is forward-equivalent). Hedging an option with actual stock injects dividend risk = delta. Futures financing rate is effectively zero, lowering carry versus financing a stock hedge. Borrow cost acts like an extra dividend, embedded asymmetrically in single-stock option quotes.

principle: Express long-vol relative value through calendars/term structure: be LONG the far-dated leg and SHORT the near-dated leg (a long calendar in Bennett's convention = long vega, but note it is SHORT gamma). Favor this when the term structure is inverted/backwardated (near > far), which typically coincides with stress, and use the square-root-of-time and normalized-z framework to find the cheapest expiry to own.
mechanism: Variance is additive, so forward vol can be stripped from two expiries. Back-month implieds overreact (Stein 1989: empirical proportionality ~0.95 vs theoretical ~0.85), so when front vol spikes the back month becomes rich (sell it); when front vol is depressed and the curve overreacts down, the back month is cheap to own. A 1x1 calendar is ~constant if surfaces move in sqrt(time), isolating the curve shape from the vol level. BS-robustness favors spread structures over outright premium.

principle: Recognize that long downside skew (OTM index puts, risk reversals) is structurally overpriced and bleeds 'skew theta' in all normal regimes; it only profits in jumpy/panic vol (realised skew > 1, i.e. ATM must move more than twice the skew per spot move). Buy index puts/long skew as deliberately-paid crash insurance, sized small — not as an expected-profit engine.
mechanism: OTM puts carry higher IV per unit gamma (skew theta = extra cost of gamma vs ATM). Under sticky local vol the favorable surface re-mark on a decline exactly cancels skew theta (break-even); only in jumpy vol does the re-mark exceed it. Market skew sits above and steeper than fair theoretical skew due to hedging/annuity demand. Index skew exceeds single-stock skew because implied correlation spikes to ~100% in crashes.

principle: Tail hedging via long index puts is a poor, expensive, leaky hedge: long-vol/variance has only ~-70% correlation to equities, costs the variance + equity-risk premium, and even including a 1987-scale crash has been historically unprofitable as a standalone. Budget tail hedges to a strict cost cap (~1-2% of portfolio/yr), prefer buying convexity when calm is just breaking (not at peak fear), and demand a statistical mispricing — not a crash narrative.
mechanism: Annualized rolling cost of short-dated puts is punishing (rolling 1M ATM ~28%/yr vs 8%/yr for 1Y). The premium proportionally narrows/inverts only at extreme VIX, so owning vol INTO a breaking panic can pay while buying at peak fear overpays. A pure long-put hedge is benchmarked against futures (which lack convexity) — only buy options when you specifically want the accelerating crash payoff. Buyers of far-OTM options generally lose (narrative fallacy).

principle: The long leg of dispersion — long single-stock vol, short index vol — is the structurally favored long-vol expression because single-stock vol carries little/no variance premium while index implied correlation (and thus index vol) is bid above fair value. But dispersion is SHORT correlation: it loses badly in crashes when correlations gap to ~1, so it is not a tail hedge. Weight legs vega-weighted (index-vs-single-stock) for cleaner correlation-mispricing exposure.
mechanism: Index variance = weighted constituent variance + correlation cross-terms; structured-product/worst-of flow lifts implied correlation above realized (~5-10 pts premium). Selling rich index vol funds cheap single-stock vol. Theta-weighting gives pure correlation exposure but is very short gamma; vega-weighting embeds an extra long single-stock vol position (a partial hedge). Dispersion is short volga, so fair implied correlation sits above realized — the gap is not free.

principle: Run long-vol as a volatility pair/relative-value trade between two correlated underlyings (e.g. long the leg near the bottom of its own IV range, short the leg near the top), to neutralize the variance-premium carry on the long leg with the short leg. Judge legs by each name's position WITHIN its own IV range (the spread), not absolute IV level; theta-weight same-type pairs (two indices), vega-weight index-vs-single-stock pairs.
mechanism: Two correlated assets share a market component plus mean-reverting noise; the spread reverts if correlation is strong enough. P&L has two drivers — implied-vol mean reversion and carry (realized vs implied). The short leg finances the long leg's theta. Weighting (theta vs vega) determines which Greek nets to zero and the residual gamma/vega sign.

principle: Size a long-vol book at a FRACTION of Kelly (e.g. half or quarter), using f = r/sigma^2 (return over the risk-free rate divided by variance) as the full-Kelly anchor, and define 'bankroll' as total net worth with a small multiplier — never broker margin/haircut. Full Kelly carries a ~1/3 chance of halving before doubling; long-vol's lumpy, autocorrelated, fat-tailed P&L violates Kelly's i.i.d. assumption, so run well below full.
mechanism: Log-utility Kelly maximizes compound growth and avoids ruin, but is extremely volatile and its benefits are only asymptotic (decades). Overbetting past 2x Kelly turns growth negative. Win-rate estimates from small samples bias Kelly too high (use the (w+1)/(N+2) shrinkage); a string of 'wins' can be largely noise. Loss aversion / prospect theory mathematically reproduces fractional Kelly. NSE short rates are high, raising the bar an edge must clear (net out the risk-free rate).

principle: Do not use arbitrary P&L stops on long-vol positions: vol moving against you (IV bleeding) is not proof you are wrong, and high vol is transient/mean-reverting. Exit only when the THESIS is invalidated (catalyst passed, forecast changed, regime shift), and control risk at entry via small sizing so the worst-case bleed is survivable. Re-evaluate edge daily using current forecast, current IV, and remaining vega.
mechanism: For a mean-reverting/cheap-vol entry, an adverse mark often coincides with MAXIMUM remaining edge; a loss stop forces you out exactly then. Risk must be controlled by sizing, not stops. Myopia cure: ask 'would I enter at current levels?' ignoring sunk P&L. But beware the IOC-style fundamental shift — huge volume + no news + extreme move = regime change, where adverse movement DOES mean you're wrong.

principle: Treat cost-minimizing hedging and execution as a first-class part of the long-vol strategy: minimize roll frequency, hedge less often (wide long-gamma bands), use limit orders to earn rather than pay the spread, prefer liquid ATM index strikes over cheap illiquid wings, and always square off winning options in the market rather than letting ITM options be exercised (to dodge punitive intrinsic-value STT). Build a custom LEAN FeeModel with STT/exchange/SEBI/GST/stamp duty or backtests will flatter long-vol returns.
mechanism: Cumulative, individually-small hedging/spread costs can exceed the perceived IV edge and are psychologically invisible because they accrue slowly. Leland adjusted-vol shows a 10%-vol illiquid name with 1% spread needs ~5.9 extra vol points just to cover daily-hedge cost. On NSE, STT (charged on sell premium, or on intrinsic value if exercised) is the dominant levy and roughly tripled 2024-2026; round trips run ~0.6% (ATM) to 2%+ (cheap tails where flat brokerage dominates).

principle: For event/jump exposure, do NOT rely on dynamic delta hedging (which cannot replicate gaps) — own the jump via long options and shape risk with STATIC option structures (calendars, wings). As a long-vol/tail trader, jump exposure IS the trade: do not hedge away the convexity you paid for. Size each event position small and cap tail risk on any short legs with far-OTM longs.
mechanism: Delta hedging assumes continuous paths; a gap leaves the hedge stale and the (1/2)Gamma*dS^2 approximation invalid. Jump risk is fundamentally an options-vs-options problem. A long option captures the full gap a delta-hedger cannot replicate. Static option hedges alone can offset jump risk. Most stock kurtosis is overnight/earnings-driven.

principle: Distinguish capturing the PRE-event IV ramp from holding THROUGH the event: front-month IV almost always ramps into a scheduled event then crushes after. The robust long-vol play is to buy ahead and EXIT before the release (monetize the vega ramp + gamma, avoid the crush). Holding a long straddle through a hyped/telegraphed catalyst usually means overpaying and eating the post-event IV collapse.
mechanism: Uncertainty about the unreleased news lifts front-month IV as the event approaches; once resolved, IV deflates regardless of the spot move. Rising IV alone does not raise option price — you need vega + gamma to beat theta. Hyped, two-sided directional demand overprices both wings. Availability bias makes post-memorable-jump straddles especially overpriced.

principle: Build the realized-vol input from a battery of estimators (close-to-close plus OHLC range estimators Parkinson/Garman-Klass/Rogers-Satchell/Yang-Zhang) over a regime-appropriate window, applying bias corrections, and treat the result as a noisy point estimate (30-day HV can be ±25%). Use Yang-Zhang for small windows / gap-prone names and close-to-close for swap-settled/close-only-hedged P&L; cap windows at ~8 months for mean reversion.
mechanism: Close-to-close is data-inefficient (variance ~sigma^2/2N); range estimators are 5-14x more efficient but biased low from discrete sampling and degrade under jump dominance. On real data the estimators converge (0.91-0.99 correlated), so estimator choice rarely makes/breaks a vol edge — effort is better spent on the forecast and catalyst. Yang-Zhang uniquely captures overnight gaps; close-to-close overestimates via gaps, range estimators underestimate.

Microstructure caveat: cap sampling at ~15-30 min on wider-spread NSE names to avoid bid-ask-bounce inflation.

principle: Use orthogonal confirmation filters for long-vol entries: volume surge to confirm a genuine vol pickup (reduce false signals), and fundamental screens (high R&D / volatile cash flow / earnings management names where the market under-forecasts vol) to pick which NSE single names to own vol on. Avoid buying vol on large, high-ROA, highly-levered names where the market over-prices vol.
mechanism: Volume is strongly correlated with all vol measures, so a vol spike on surging volume is more likely genuine and persistent (clustering). Fundamentals predict vol with an orthogonal, less-crowded signal suited to longer horizons and relative (cross-stock) ranking; the high-leverage exception (market over-prices its vol) is because bond markets understand the risk better than the vol market.

principle: Evaluate a long-vol program with a PANEL of metrics (Sharpe + Sortino + Calmar + K), not Sharpe alone: Sharpe penalizes the upside (positive-skew) volatility that long vol is built on, and short-vol's high Sharpe is a peso-problem illusion hiding tail risk. Use Sortino (downside-only) to fairly credit long vol's convex upside; expect a low/negative short-window Sharpe (its standard error ~equals its value over 100 days) and don't read it as edge decay.
mechanism: Long-vol equity curves are positively skewed (many small theta losses, rare large gains); large gains inflate Sharpe's standard-deviation denominator and depress it, even though that variance is desirable. Short-OTM-selling manufactures double-digit Sharpe by hiding rare large losses. No single metric captures performance; each mishandles skew differently. Calmar (~1 rule) ties return target to survivable drawdown.

Sinclair Ch.9 p.172-173 (Sharpe sampling error ~ its own magnitude)
Bennett 1.4 p.21-22 (overwriting low std-dev hides unchanged downside — use Sortino)
Sinclair Ch.9 p.166-167 (record stats per underlying, segment long-vol vs short-vol)
conflicting_citations: Sinclair Ch.9 p.174-175 (Omega/K ratio dominated by rare tail observations — don't over-trust on a long-vol book)
Sinclair Ch.9 p.180-181 (long-vol lumpy returns hard to lever safely under consistency criteria)

prelim_nse_qc_fit: Metric panel is pure post-trade analysis, fully implementable on any P&L series including a self-built NSE backtest. Especially important because a long-vol NSE book will show poor short-window Sharpe (bleed + rare payoff). Record per-underlying and segment long-vol vs short-vol. The small India sample/regime history makes peso-problem and small-sample caveats acute.

principle: Pre-commit money-management parameters and three benchmark levels (success / respectability / failure / max-bleed budget) BEFORE trading, because a long-vol book spends almost all its time in drawdown (continuous theta bleed). Set the failure/liquidation threshold so cumulative bleed never forces an exit before the convex payoff; do not slash size on a normal run of bleed days or up-size after a lucky tail win.
mechanism: Drawdowns are the dominant survival risk; you don't get paid recovering lost ground. Sizing decisions compound multiplicatively and can dominate trade selection. Pre-committed benchmarks guard against over-management and selective-memory bias. Long-vol's high loss-day frequency means a normal bleed streak is expected, not a signal; arbitrary size cuts also amplify fixed-cost drag.

The Rs 15-20 lakh contract size and coarse lots make sizing decisions chunky and high-stakes for retail. For pure long-premium positions, max loss per position is bounded (the premium), which helps define the failure budget cleanly.

principle: Pre-accept that long-vol positions lose money most days even when eventually profitable; the daily theta-drip is a behavioral trap, not a sell signal. Counter the systematic pull toward the (worse-expectation but psychologically seductive) short-vol side by writing a pretrade plan (forecast, edge in vol points, size, exit triggers) and judging the process, not single outcomes.
mechanism: Short-term thinking and loss aversion make the steady drip of small losses on long gamma feel worse than the rare large gain feels good, biasing traders toward short-vol's steady-small-gains/rare-disaster profile. Loss aversion also sustains the IV premium (buyers over-value capped-loss payoffs). Prospect theory's convex-gains/concave-losses value function makes people snatch small option gains too early — killing the let-convexity-run payoff long vol depends on.

prelim_nse_qc_fit: Behavioral discipline is universal; for a retail NSE trader running long vol on QC/broker, a written pretrade plan and process-based evaluation are the main defense against capitulating during the (frequent) bleed days inherent to India's positive-VRP, mean-reverting-vol environment. No data constraint; this is operating discipline layered on the strategy.

principle: Concentrate long-vol capital where edge is capturable and execution is clean: liquid NIFTY index options for the long-gamma/long-vega core and short-dated weekly gamma, accepting that this is also the most professionally-contested market; treat thin single-stock/mid-cap NSE options as event-only, hold-to-payoff vehicles. Product selection and execution quality are plausibly more important than the vol model itself.
mechanism: Edge persists where there is end-user flow and weak competition, but capturable edge also requires tight spreads and depth — a tension on NSE where liquidity (NIFTY) and the least-contested niches (thin single names) are at opposite ends. Execution (limit orders, instrument liquidity, avoiding spread-crossing) determines whether a theoretical IV edge is realized. Buying-only retail can sit on bid/offer for the long-gamma hedge.

Single-stock options (~10% turnover, ~1.2% spreads, ~10% on thin names) are event-only. QC cannot trade either natively; execution must be via broker API with careful limit-order use. The binding constraint is platform/data, not instrument choice.

principle: Accept that QuantConnect is at best a research/modeling engine for NSE long vol, not a turnkey stack: it provides NO NSE options/index/futures data and supports India only for LIVE EQUITY trading (Zerodha/Samco). Any NSE options long-vol strategy requires self-sourced/reformatted NSE options data for backtesting and a separate live-execution path (e.g. direct broker API), with QC limited to the equity hedge leg. A turnkey QC long-vol stack would instead use US SPX/SPY/VIX options.
mechanism: NSE data-licensing prevents QC from shipping India options/index/futures history; LEAN's option machinery (pricing models, Greeks/IV, fee/fill models, code-driven delta-hedging) is solid and exchange-agnostic but produces no option chain without bundled data. Native custom-data ingestion models a single TIME/VALUE series and does not reconstruct a full strikes/expiries/Greeks universe automatically.

principle: Monitor whether the long-vol edge is being competed away or structurally changed: watch for the earnings-jump/skew/variance premia richening over time (crowding), shifts in counterparties (retail vs professional flow), and regulatory/structural breaks (SEBI 2024-25 reforms). When a winning vol trade degrades, diagnose whether the source of edge disappeared, execution declined, or the counterparty mix changed — and reframe book-strategies that don't translate (var swaps, VIX ETNs, exotics) into the closest retail-accessible NSE expression.
mechanism: Trading competes away edge; structural changes (tick size, expiry rationalization, lot-size hikes, settlement rules) can remove a mispricing entirely. The variance-premium/event-vol edges that fund long vol can crowd out. Many book strategies (variance swaps, corridor/gamma swaps, VIX ETNs, worst-of/exotics, forward-starts) are OTC/unavailable to NSE retail and must be reframed: var swap -> 1/K^2 NIFTY option strip; VIX long -> NIFTY straddles; dispersion -> long single-stock straddles vs short index, where liquidity permits.

principle: Where you want spot-robust, path-independent long realized-variance exposure (constant cash gamma that doesn't decay as spot drifts from a single strike), approximate a variance swap with a 1/K^2-weighted strip of listed NIFTY options (heavily weighted toward low-strike puts) plus delta hedging. Recognize this strip is automatically ~60% puts, long skew, and long volatility-surface curvature — and that variance pays MORE than linear vol on large moves (convex tail bonus).
mechanism: A single long option loses gamma as spot moves away from its strike; a 1/K^2 strip holds constant dollar-gamma across spot (the log-contract). Requiring vega invariance to spot yields w(K)=const/K^2. Variance > gamma swap > vol swap by Jensen (convexity/vol-of-vol premium); the variance payoff's quadratic term blows up favorably in big moves — exactly the tail behavior a long-vol trader wants.

Bennett A.5 p.250-251 (variance notional = vega/2sigma)
Bennett 2.3 p.55 (var vs vol payoff diverges sharply on large moves)
conflicting_citations: Bennett 2.3 p.54 (deep-OTM wings not listed/liquid — truncated strip leaves you short tail risk / mis-replicated)

prelim_nse_qc_fit: Conceptually the cleanest retail substitute for an (unavailable) NSE variance swap, but the strip needs many liquid strikes including OTM puts — feasible only on NIFTX where strikes are dense, and even there the far-OTM wings are thin/wide, so the strip is truncated (mis-replicated, short tail). Heavy 1/K^2 weighting on low strikes means most cost is in the richly-skewed NIFTY puts. Delta hedging requires NIFTY futures (no QC data). Costs (STT, spreads) on a multi-strike strip are substantial.

principle: Understand (and avoid being silently short vol via) embedded leverage: leveraged/inverse ETFs carry a deterministic short-realized-variance drag exp(-0.5*lambda*(lambda-1)*sigma^2*t), and a fixed-leverage position is a Kelly over-bet whenever realized vol is high (critical leverage ~drift/vol^2). Convexity in price does NOT imply long variance — a structure can look straddle-like yet still be short realized variance and bleed in chop.
mechanism: Daily rebalancing to fixed leverage loses on equal up/down moves ((1+x)(1-x)<1), amplified by leverage; this is an explicit short-variance/theta term. The same high-sigma^2 condition that makes a leveraged ETF bleed is what makes long gamma profitable — they are mirror images. A leveraged ETF is effectively a perpetual power option on the reference.
supporting_citations: Sinclair Ch.13 p.231-237 (volatility drag = short-variance term; leverage as Kelly over-bet; power-option/long-short structures)
Sinclair Ch.13 p.234-235 (long-short ETF/reference replicates a straddle that is SHORT realized variance — convexity-in-price != long variance)
Sinclair Ch.8 p.157-158 (fat tails break the OU/log-optimal sizing rule)
conflicting_citations: Sinclair Ch.13 p.232-233 (naive-leverage term can dominate the drag — leveraged ETF can OVERPERFORM, so the drag isn't always the dominant effect)
prelim_nse_qc_fit: Largely informational for NSE retail: India has essentially no liquid leveraged/inverse equity ETFs and no India VIX ETPs, so the direct trade is unavailable. The transferable lesson is the warning — do not mistake price-convexity for long variance, and recognize any embedded-leverage holding carries a hidden short-vol drag. The Carr-Madan spanning identity (replicate any nonlinear payoff from index puts/calls) is reusable on NIFTX. Not a primary candidate for an NSE long-vol program.
